text:
# Page 1^page=1
MOM_CARE Project Handoff: Current Known State P repared for transfer into a new memory-enabled project Generated May 6, 2026 from available project conversation context Use This First ● Purp o se: This document consolidates the current visible MOM_CARE project context so the next project can start with continuity instead of chaos. ● Imp o rtan t limitatio n : This is not a verified medical chart. It is a caregiving operations summary based on conversation history, photos mentioned in prior chats, and caregiver-reported observations. Medication names, doses, and timing must be reconciled against pill bottles, discharge papers, pharmacy records, and clinician instructions. ● Primary p atien t: Cody's mother, referenced in project notes as Lisa English / Lisa-English in database-style entries. ● Primary caregiver/o p erato r: Cody. Cody is coordinating meds, shopping, transport, documentation, caseworker/Humana reporting, app/database work, and day-to-day safety/reassurance. Executive Sn ap sho t ● Patient has significant chronic medical complexity with respiratory disease, oxygen needs, pain flares, mobility limitations, transfer/fall risk, medication-management burden, and periods of confusion/denial/frustration during stressful care moments. ● Recent trend described by caregiver: prognosis is stable and showing meaningful potential for improvement with caregiver support; demeanor/cognitive engagement has improved compared with more chaotic earlier periods. ● Care environment recently changed after a move. Humidity/indoor comfort concerns were noted in the new house; patient is closer to river area and home feels damp despite moderate thermostat temperature. ● Major operating need: convert caregiving from memory-dependent chaos into visible, checkable systems: med matrix, bedside tracker, task ledger, supplies map, transfer/safety protocol, and medical/caseworker summary packet. C urren t Prio rities / Triage Priority Why It Matters Current Action / System Need Medication reconciliation and schedule control Multiple meds, PRNs, antibiotics/steroids/opioids/pain meds, respiratory treatments, and injection timing create high risk of missed/double doses. Use printed matrix with meds on Y-axis and time windows/hours on X-axis; reconcile with bottles and clinical orders; label bottles by risk/time category. Respiratory monitoring Oxygen readings around low 90s were reported; breathing treatments and oxygen concentrator use are active concerns. Track O2, symptoms, oxygen flow setting, treatment times, and red flags. Escalate per clinician/emergency criteria, not vibes. Pain positioning and mobility Knee/back/shoulder pain, couch/bed alignment problems, transfer difficulty, and fall risk are recurring. Keep torso/head aligned, avoid bowed couch positioning, use ice/cold pack when appropriate, document what position worsens/improves pain. Emotional safety and cooperation Patient may resist, deny, or educate/argue when feeling controlled. Care works better when Cody frames actions as her choice and visible support. Use short reassurance loops: “You’re in control. I’m doing this because you asked. I’m checking details so we keep you safe.”

# Page 2^page=2
Caregiver operational load Cody is carrying logistics, meds, app build, housing/money , caseworker communications, and emotional labor . Centralize into one ledger: tasks, meds, supplies, calls, follow-ups, receipts, and next actions. No more scattered SMS pile. Kn o wn / Men tio n ed Medical C o n ditio n s an d Fun ctio n al C o n cern s ● Chronic respiratory disease requiring oxygen support and breathing treatments. Dupixent injections indicate a significant inflammatory/allergic/asthma/COPD-adjacent respiratory or immune condition, but the exact diagnosis should be verified. ● Mobility impairment requiring wheelchair/power chair considerations, bedside commode/porta-potty transfers, transfer board discussion, and wheelchair-accessible medical taxi planning. ● Pain issues involving knee pain, back pain, shoulder positioning, nighttime pain, and medication/position-related discomfort. Left knee pain was specifically mentioned as worsening during one episode. ● Cognitive/behavioral care concern: when stressed, patient may not remember prior agreements, may enter denial/frustration, and may resist explanation. Caregiver is trying to rebuild trust/safety before introducing bigger long-term plans. ● Fall/transfer risk: hospital context, porta-potty transfer, slippery transfer device concern, need for safe transfer system, and monitoring after positioning changes. ● Blood pressure medication monitoring appears relevant based on lisinopril/metoprolol references and prior BP-medication tracking discussion. ● Blood glucose monitoring exists or was considered: McKesson True Metrix Pro glucometer Error 3 was looked up in project history. C urren t / Men tio n ed Medicatio n s an d Treatmen ts Reconcile this table against current bottles, discharge paperwork, pharmacy list, and clinician instructions. Items below are “known mentioned,” not guaranteed active. Medication / Treatment Known Details from Project Context Status / Schedule Notes Risk / Follow-up Dupixent injectable Dose administered into right side of belly. Next dose noted as due in 14 days from that event. Event recorded April 17, 2026 in project history; biweekly schedule implied. Confirm exact last administered date, dose strength, injection site rotation, storage, and next due date. Prednisone 5 mg Database-style starter record mentioned taper: 3 tabs/day x 5 days from transcript, started around 2026-03-01; Cody later noted prednisone timing evenings after ~5:30 PM; later considered stopping because pain seemed to return badly after taking it. May be active, recently active, or discontinued depending on clinician plan. Evening timing was specifically requested to update record. Do not stop/change steroid without clinician guidance unless already instructed. Verify current plan; steroid changes can matter. Morphine Mentioned as a high-risk medication needing red label / “make sure doing it right.” Schedule/dose not visible in available context. High risk: sedation, breathing suppression, falls, confusion, constipation, interactions. Must reconcile. Hydrocodone Used for pain; patient said it wore off. Timing “earlier today” in one episode. PRN pain medication likely; exact dose and interval not visible. Avoid stacking with morphine or sedating meds unless prescribed. Track last dose/time. Gabapentin 600 mg - RX7005388 Starter inventory record said baseline full bottle, PRN pain, no change yet. PRN pain per starter record. Can cause sedation/dizziness/falls. Track use and effect. Metoprolol ER Starter record: approx 2 tablets left as of 2026-04-10; Likely daily cardiovascular/BP medication. Needs refill/inventory check if not already resolved.

# Page 3^page=3
daily use approx count update -2. Lisinopril Mentioned as regular daily medicine. Likely daily BP medication. Track BP and potassium/renal considerations per clinician. Omeprazole Mentioned as regular daily medicine. Likely daily GI protection/reflux med. Low urgency but should be on matrix. Potassium Added per case update / Humana safe report discussion. Active addition reported April 22, 2026. Confirm dose, frequency , lab follow-up, and relation to lisinopril or diuretics. Potassium mistakes can matter. Sulfamethoxazole / antibiotic Project title referenced “Sulfamethoxazole vs Opioids.” Exact active status unclear . Reconcile antibiotic course, start/end date, and interaction cautions. Mucinex Cody was shopping for Mucinex at Walmart. Likely symptomatic respiratory/congestion support. Confirm formulation and avoid duplicate ingredients. Ipratropium bromide nasal spray Discussed in project history. Status unclear . Confirm active use and directions. Ibuprofen / Tylenol / Naproxen Patient reported ibuprofen/T ylenol not helping; naproxen was discussed. OTC pain options considered. NSAIDs can interact with BP/kidneys/steroids/blood thinners/GI risk. Verify clinician permission. Breathing treatments Patient was in hospital and receiving medication and breathing treatments; home treatment timing every 4 hours was discussed in app-building context. May be q4h or PRN depending plan. Track exact medication, interval, O2 response, and symptoms. Oxygen concentrator At one point concentrator plugged in and set around 2.2; oxygen steady near 91-92 with transient dips. Ongoing oxygen support implied. Verify prescribed flow rate, acceptable range, escalation thresholds, and equipment status. Medicatio n Labelin g / Organ izatio n System A lready Started ● Cody bought or planned to buy 315 color-coded labels. ● Initial labeling concept: red = high caution / verify carefully, green = routine daily meds. Also considered letters like D = day, N = noon/night, B = bedtime. ● Cody organized pill bottles in front of patient so she could see what was happening and feel safer/included. ● Needed next step: standardize labels into a simple schema before it becomes rainbow chaos with stickers. Suggested: Red = high-risk/controlled/acute change; Yellow = PRN or conditional; Green = daily routine; Blue = respiratory; Purple = injectable/special schedule; White label = exact time/window and “last dose tracked on matrix.” C urren t Mo n ito rin g an d B edside Tracker Needs Tracker Area Fields to Capture Notes Medication taken Medication, dose, scheduled time, actual time, initials/check, reason if skipped/refused, PRN reason, effect after 30-90 min. Core paper matrix needed. Patient can check off. Cody needs a fail-safe. Oxygen / breathing O2 %, pulse, oxygen flow, breathing treatment time, cough/congestion, shortness of breath, sleep/wake state. Especially important because readings around 89-92 were observed. Pain Location, 0-10 score, position, medication/ice/heat used, effect, triggers. Separate knee/back/shoulder if needed.

# Page 4^page=4
Transfers From/to surface, assist needed, device used, problems/slips, confidence, near-fall. Use for learning safer transfer setup. Food/fluids Meals, fluids, caffeine, nausea, bowel movement if opioids active. Coca-Cola/cof fee/caf feine intake came up; hydration and constipation matter. Mood/cooperation Calm/anxious/frustrated/confused, what reassurance worked. This is operationally important, not judgment. Key C aregivin g C o mmun icatio n Strategy ● Do not lead with “you are wrong” or a big explanation when she is defensive. It triggers denial/frustration and she may try to educate Cody instead of processing the plan. ● Frame support as her control: “I’m doing this because you asked me to,” “It’s your choice,” “I’m not disagreeing, I’m checking so we can rule things out and keep you safe.” This has already worked once to calm her down. ● Build safety before strategy. Visible caregiving actions help: making food, bringing drink in spill-proof container, organizing meds in front of her, using calm narration, and not surprising her with abstract plans. ● For long-term planning, wait until she is fed, calm, medicated appropriately, and feels safe. Then explain one small next step, not the whole empire. Even ts / Timelin e C ap tured in Pro ject C o n text Approx Date Event / Observation Why It Matters Mar 1, 2026 approx Prednisone taper referenced as started from transcript; starter record showed 3 tabs/day x 5 days. Medication history / possible steroid course. Apr 10, 2026 Starter inventory/activity entries for Metoprolol ER, Prednisone 5mg, Gabapentin 600mg, and other meds began. Cody wanted patient activity table considered for Supabase. Start of structured medication/inventory tracking effort. Apr 10, 2026 Need for “paper app” bedside tracker like hospital room summary; urgency around making caregiving system functional ASAP. Origin of printed tracker / app concept. Apr 11, 2026 iCloud family sharing / backup for mom’s phone successfully updated to use 2TB family storage; phone backup completed. Digital continuity and device stability. Apr 12, 2026 Explored wheelchair taxi / med taxi options in Evansville, Indiana for power chair transport. Transportation barrier / mobility logistics. Apr 15-16, 2026 Oxygen concentrator on around 2.2; O2 steady about 91-92 with brief dips around 89 during exhale, then recovery; humidity in new house suspected. Respiratory monitoring and environmental comfort. Apr 16, 2026 Shopping for reusable cold packs/ice bag. Medium back/body cold compress 50% off for $8 was identified; knee-specific option was $16. Pain management supplies. Apr 17, 2026 Dupixent injection administered right abdomen; transfer to porta-potty successful but transfer device felt slippery; next Dupixent due in 14 days. Injection schedule and transfer safety learning. Apr 17, 2026 Prednisone timing updated to evenings after approximately 5:30 PM. Medication schedule correction. Apr 18, 2026 Pain/positioning assessment: couch dip causing bowed back; prior bed had head-down/legs-above-head incline; suggestion to align torso/head with pillows while moving bed to test. Positioning likely affects back/shoulder pain; systematic testing needed.

# Page 5^page=5
Apr 21, 2026 Medication matrix idea: x-axis time of day/hours, y-axis meds/doses, printed check-off schedule. Core med adherence system design. Apr 22, 2026 Requested current state case update for caseworker and Humana safe report; potassium added; prognosis language adjusted to stable/improving with caregiver support. External reporting / case management documentation. Apr 28, 2026 Hospital context: medication and breathing treatments; one abnormal reading noted but other readings okay and staff expected to recheck. Acute episode / hospital monitoring. May 6, 2026 Cody at Walmart gathering supplies, labels, Mucinex, food/comfort items; plan to make sandwich, donuts as treat, Coke with ice in spill-proof container , organize pill bottles visibly. Immediate stabilization by meeting needs and restoring safety/trust. C o mp leted / In Pro gress C aregivin g In frastructure ● C o mp leted o r started: iCloud family storage/backup, some medication inventory starter records, Dupixent administration record, transfer attempt/learning, cold-pack shopping, label system purchase/plan, caseworker/Humana report drafting, transportation research, medication-matrix design discussion. ● In p ro gress: Supabase/app schema for patient activity, medication inventory, medication schedules, PRN tracking, and voice-activated PWA concept for patient/caregiver use. ● Still n eeded: One canonical medication list, printed check-off matrix, refill tracker, escalation plan, supplies inventory, appointment/caseworker calendar, shared task ledger, and patient-friendly reassurance board/dry erase substitute. D igital / A p p / D atabase System C o n cep ts A lready D iscussed ● Cody wants a PWA/app the patient can use on her phone, ideally voice-activated, to track medication timers, treatments, vitals, PRNs, and care actions. ● Supabase table concern: “patient_activity” existed in spreadsheet with fields: log_id, patient_id, medication_id, inventory_id, action, quantity_change, reason, notes, created_at. Cody questioned whether it should be merged or added to Supabase. ● Current recommendation from project context: keep patient_activity as an event ledger rather than merging into static medication table. It is the audit trail: doses taken, count updates, refills, PRNs, missed doses, taper changes, symptom-linked actions. ● Paper-first remains critical. App can fail; paper matrix on clipboard/bedside can be used immediately and later entered into the database. Ho me En viro n men t / Eq uip men t / Sup p lies Category Known Items / Needs Notes Respiratory Oxygen concentrator , water/humidification check, breathing treatments, Mucinex. Track equipment sounds, flow rate, O2 response. Humidity in house may affect comfort. Mobility / Transfers Power chair, porta-potty/bedside commode, possible transfer board, wheelchair med taxi. Transfer board may be slippery; reassess safer method before repeated use. Pain / Comfort Reusable cold pack, knee-specific cold/hot compress considered, pillows for alignment. Avoid heat if patient refuses or if not clinically appropriate. Medication Organization Color-coded labels, pill bottles, printed matrix/checklist. Use visible organization to reduce patient anxiety.

# Page 6^page=6
Food / Trust Reset Sandwich, donuts as treat, Coca-Cola with ice in spill-proof cup/straw. Not nutrition perfection; this is immediate cooperation/safety stabilization. Use strategically. Communication Board Dry erase board desired but not found. Use paper clipboard or laminated sheet as substitute. Kn o wn C o n cern s / Op en Lo o p s ● Exact active medication list, doses, prescribers, and timing are not fully reconciled in available context. ● Whether prednisone is currently active, stopped, or should be stopped is unclear and clinically important. ● Potassium dose and reason need confirmation, especially with BP meds and any kidney/lab concerns. ● Exact oxygen prescription, target O2 range, and escalation thresholds need confirmation from discharge/doctor instructions. ● Opioid plan needs clarity: morphine vs hydrocodone timing, maximum daily dose, sedation checks, constipation plan, and when to call clinician. ● Transfer safety needs a repeatable protocol: surfaces, brakes, gait belt/board/no board, footwear, commode placement, and what counts as “do not attempt.” ● Humidity/dampness in new house may need dehumidifier/filter/HVAC evaluation, especially if respiratory symptoms worsen. ● Task overload from mom’s messages needs a single shared task ledger. iPhone-native options were discussed; simplest likely shared Apple Reminders list or shared Notes before Notion complexity. Suggested Next 72-Ho ur Op eratin g Plan Order Action Definition of Done 1 Create one-page current medication list from bottles/discharge papers. Each med has name, strength, dose, time/window , reason, prescriber , PRN limits, last filled, refill need. 2 Print bedside medication/vitals matrix. Patient/caregiver can check off doses and record O2/pulse/pain without opening an app. 3 Build red/yellow/green/blue label schema and apply consistently . No duplicate meanings. High-risk meds clearly marked. 4 Make a transfer safety card. Steps visible near commode/bed; includes “stop and call for help” conditions. 5 Set Dupixent next-dose reminder and injection log. Last dose, next due, site rotation, lot/notes if available. 6 Start shared task ledger . All incoming mom requests go into one list with status: capture, triage, doing, waiting, done. 7 Prepare caseworker/Humana update packet. Stable/improving language, clinical details, equipment/transport needs, caregiver support documented. Reco mmen ded D aily C are Rhythm ● Mo rn in g reset: Food/drink, oxygen/vitals check, med matrix review, pain score, transfer needs, environment check. ● Midday check: Breathing treatment if scheduled, PRN review, hydration/food, task ledger update, supply check. ● Even in g reset: Prednisone timing if still active and ordered, pain/positioning setup, oxygen equipment check, next-day med prep. ● B efo re exp lain in g p lan s: Make sure she is fed, comfortable, calm, and sees Cody helping rather than controlling. Then explain one next step only.

# Page 7^page=7
Patien t Reassuran ce Scrip t B an k ● “You are safe. I’m not taking over; I’m helping you carry it.” ● “I’m doing this because you asked me. You’re still in control.” ● “I’m not arguing with you. I’m checking the details so we don’t miss anything important.” ● “Let’s do the next tiny thing first. We don’t have to solve the whole thing right now.” ● “I’m going to write it down so neither of us has to remember it from memory.” New Pro ject In structio n s / C o n text to Paste Paste this section into the new memory-enabled project as durable context: Cody is caregiving for his mother, Lisa English / Lisa-English, who has complex respiratory , mobility, pain, and medication-management needs. The operating goal is to turn caregiving from memory-dependent chaos into simple visible systems: printed med matrix, bedside tracker, color-coded bottles, task ledger , transfer protocol, and caseworker-ready clinical summaries. Patient cooperation improves when she feels in control and sees visible acts of care before big explanations. She may resist or deny when stressed; use reassurance and one-step framing. Current known meds/treatments mentioned include Dupixent q14 days, prednisone timing after ~5:30 PM if active, morphine, hydrocodone, gabapentin 600mg PRN, metoprolol ER, lisinopril, omeprazole, potassium, possible sulfamethoxazole, Mucinex, ipratropium nasal spray, breathing treatments, oxygen concentrator , and OTC pain meds under caution. This list must be reconciled against real bottles and clinician instructions. Key risks: respiratory decline, low O2, opioid/sedation/fall risk, steroid/potassium/BP-med interactions, transfer falls, pain-positioning cycles, caregiver overload. Recent trend: stable with meaningful improvement potential under Cody’s support. Prioritize systems, clarity, and fast triage. No n -Nego tiable Safety No tes ● This document cannot replace clinician instructions. Use it as an operations map, not a medication authority. ● Any severe shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, severe confusion, fainting, suspected overdose, fall with injury, or oxygen levels outside clinician/emergency thresholds should be escalated immediately. ● Do not make medication changes based solely on observed patterns without checking the prescribing clinician/pharmacist, especially for prednisone, opioids, potassium, BP meds, antibiotics, or respiratory medications. E nd of handoff document
